# Questions in Livestream 209

Live Stream #209: One-Meaning Flippancy

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:40</a> | If the exodus happened under Ramesses II, why did he remain highly esteemed instead of being remembered as a pharaoh who failed to preserve *ma'at*? | He argues the exodus would have happened only about halfway through Ramesses II's long reign, leaving decades for image repair through later monuments, temples, and royal memory-shaping. | He frames the question around Ramesses II's unusually long reign. If the exodus occurred around the middle of that reign, Ramesses still had many years to build, campaign, sponsor temples, and shape public memory. Egyptian royal reputation was not a neutral historical ledger; monuments and later court memory could emphasize success, piety, and order while suppressing humiliation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=738" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:18</a> | Were the Egyptian gods originally based on humans who were later deified? | Generally no; he says the gods do not appear to be former humans, apart from a few later back-attributions tied to tomb traditions. | He rejects a general euhemerist explanation for Egyptian deities. In his account, Egyptian gods are not normally remembered humans who gradually became divine. A few later traditions can attach divine status or origins to remembered tomb figures, but those are exceptions and do not explain the major gods as a class. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=832" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:52</a> | Weren't all pharaohs gods in a sense? | Not in a simple sense; he distinguishes Egyptian apotheosis and royal/divine status from saying every pharaoh was originally a god. | He separates royal divinity from the claim that every pharaoh was simply a god in the same way as the gods of the pantheon. Pharaohs could have divine status, divine offices, or posthumous apotheosis, but that is not the same as saying the gods were once human kings. The category is more complicated than a flat identification of kings with gods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=1165" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:25</a> | How should the famous "helicopter," "jet," and "light bulb" carvings be explained? | He says the "helicopter" and "jet" are palimpsests, with one inscription carved over another, and the "light bulb" is a stylized lotus, not evidence of advanced ancient technology. | He treats the Abydos machine-looking shapes as overwritten inscriptions, where older and newer hieroglyphic signs combine visually after recarving and wear. The Dendera "light bulb" imagery is explained as a lotus motif rather than technology. His larger point is that the signs fit normal Egyptian artistic and inscriptional practice without invoking aircraft, electricity, or lost modern machinery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=1358" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:38</a> | Why is Abraham called God's friend? | He says Abraham's heart was aligned toward doing God's will and serving God willingly, even though Abraham still sinned like everyone else. | He explains the title relationally rather than as a claim that Abraham was morally flawless. Abraham is called God's friend because his will and loyalty are oriented toward God; he wants to serve God and do what God commands. That does not erase Abraham's sins, but it describes the basic posture of faith and obedience attributed to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=1501" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:01</a> | Would Abraham have worn Canaanite clothing or clothing from his ancestral homeland? | More Canaanite-style pastoral clothing, because his life as a sojourning shepherd would differ from the city life of his Mesopotamian homeland. | He reasons from Abraham's lived setting and occupation. A pastoral sojourner in Canaan would dress for the local climate, mobility, and shepherding life rather than preserve the full urban style of Mesopotamia. He therefore expects Abraham's clothing to look more like Canaanite pastoral dress than like elite city clothing from his ancestral region. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=1585" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:25</a> | Are cognate studies still a safe path for someone who wants to become an Old Testament scholar? | He says the hiring trend has shifted away from it, but he still strongly recommends cognate study because the training quality is often excellent. | He distinguishes job-market safety from educational value. The Old Testament field is not hiring as many cognate specialists as before, so it may be risky as a career strategy. Even so, he says cognate programs are often small and strong, can provide excellent training, and may be a better educational bet than some broader Hebrew Bible programs with uneven quality. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=1937" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:17</a> | Would Moses have known he was a Hebrew? | Probably not in the later ethnic sense; he would have known he was a son of Jacob, but the Hebrew identity as an ethnonym had not yet formed. | He argues that "Hebrew" is a later or complicated label connected with *hapiru*, so Moses likely would not have used it as his own ethnic self-designation. Moses could know he belonged to the people descended from Jacob, but that is not identical to the later ethnonym. The answer depends on distinguishing family or clan identity from later national terminology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2063" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:23</a> | Are all Hebrews bums, but not all bums Hebrews? | No. He distinguishes *hapiru* as a class term for outcasts or sojourners from Hebrew as a later language or ethnic designation. | He explains *hapiru* as a social category rather than an ethnic one. It can describe marginal, displaced, or dependent people, but that does not mean every such person is an Israelite or later Hebrew. "Hebrew" develops as a different kind of label, so the terms can overlap historically without being interchangeable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2260" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:40</a> | What was the pre-exodus religious practice at Avaris? | He says Avaris was dominated by Canaanite worship, especially Baal, along with deities such as Astarte and Resheph. | He describes Avaris as heavily Canaanite in religious character before the exodus. The main cultic profile he names is Baal worship, with other West Semitic deities such as Astarte and Resheph also present. That religious mix fits the delta site's population and culture better than a purely Egyptian cultic picture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2473" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:13</a> | What ancient Near Eastern peoples had seven-day weeks? | He says the clearest early example he knows is Israel; Egypt had ten-day weeks, Rome had an eight-day cycle, and Mesopotamian or Canaanite evidence is uncertain. | He treats Israel as the clearest early seven-day-week case and contrasts it with other systems. Egypt used ten-day periods, Rome had an eight-day cycle, and he does not claim firm Mesopotamian or Canaanite evidence for the same pattern. The answer is cautious because similar calendrical rhythms are easy to overstate without direct evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2587" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:07</a> | Were the people in Luke 8 Gadites rather than Greeks? | Yes. He says the region belonged to Gad, with Reuben and Manasseh nearby, so Jesus was speaking to Israelites there. | He places the episode geographically east of the Jordan in territory associated with Gad, near Reuben and Manasseh. On that basis, he understands the people in the narrative as Israelites rather than Greeks. His answer depends on tribal geography rather than on assuming a gentile population from later names alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2755" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:55</a> | What was the living situation of the Ramesside family during and after Moses's birth? | He places the Ramessides in the delta, likely around Avaris, with their position changing as dynastic politics and Moses's adoption developed. | He situates the Ramesside family in the eastern delta, probably in the Avaris area, before they came fully to royal prominence. Moses's adoption and the family's rising status would occur within changing dynastic politics, so the setting is not a static palace scene from the start. The answer is tentative but keeps the family tied to the delta power base. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=2892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:12</a> | Was Moses ever in line for the Egyptian throne? | No. He says succession required royal birth through a queen or dynastic crisis, and Moses did not qualify. | He rejects the popular idea that Moses was a plausible heir. Egyptian succession depended on legitimate royal descent, especially through a queen, or on special political crisis conditions. As an adopted outsider, Moses could be raised in the court but would not automatically enter the line for kingship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=3039" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:39</a> | Do we have any clue which son of Ramesses may have died in the plague? | He says the likeliest candidate is Amun-her-khepeshef, Ramesses II's firstborn crown prince, who disappears around the twenty-fifth regnal year. | He identifies Amun-her-khepeshef as the best candidate because he was Ramesses II's firstborn and crown prince and appears to drop out of the record around the relevant period. He presents this as a plausible historical alignment, not as a directly attested plague notice. The argument rests on chronology and the prince's disappearance from succession prominence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=3524" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:44</a> | What biblical people or places would later forgers not know about but have been verified? | He gave Pi-hahiroth as an obscure Exodus place verified in Egyptian texts and unlikely to be known by later writers. | He uses Pi-hahiroth as an example of an obscure place name that appears in the biblical exodus itinerary and is also known from Egyptian material. Because it is not an obvious or famous location, he argues it would be difficult for a much later writer to invent convincingly. The point is not that the name proves every exodus detail, but that it is a meaningful historical anchor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=4320" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:00</a> | Did the Philistines reach Mount Gilboa by penetrating the highlands or through the Jezreel Valley? | He said they entered by the Jezreel Valley, a common and easier route for armies. | He explains the movement in terms of terrain and military practicality. The Jezreel Valley provides a broad, natural route into the region, while forcing a path through the highlands would be much harder. So he reads the Philistine approach to Gilboa as valley movement rather than a difficult highland penetration. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=4423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:43</a> | Besides Thoth, were there Egyptian gods that came from body parts or fluids of other gods? | Yes. He said some gods, especially minor ones, could arise from body parts, procreation, or self-creation. | He says Egyptian myth contains multiple modes of divine origin. Some deities, particularly lesser figures, can be generated from body parts, bodily fluids, procreation, or self-creative acts. He does not make this the origin of all gods, but accepts it as a real pattern in Egyptian mythic storytelling. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=4576" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:16</a> | Since you have mentioned being in Canada for the glory of God, what are you doing up there? | He answers personally and jokingly that he lives in Vancouver, finds it expensive and frustrating, and would return to the United States if he could. | He treats this as a personal aside rather than a scholarly answer. He says he is in Vancouver, jokes about the cost and frustrations of living there, and indicates that he would prefer to return to the United States if circumstances allowed. The "for the glory of God" phrasing is handled with humor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5144" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:44</a> | How would you respond to David Bentley Hart's claim that Genesis must be spiritualized or becomes myth? | He called that a false dichotomy, since other readings exist and Genesis does not say the garden trees were for gods to eat. | He rejects the framing that Genesis must be either spiritualized or reduced to myth. He argues that the text itself does not require Hart's specific reading of the garden trees as divine food, and that other interpretive options remain available. His answer is mainly methodological: do not force the passage into a two-option choice the text does not demand. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5310" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:30</a> | Would you suggest that myth is a kind of language? | He says myth is not quite a language, but it is a kind of expression that can communicate meaning while still referring to something understood as literal. | He resists calling myth a language in a strict sense, but grants that it functions as a communicative mode. A mythic form can express meaning through story, image, and shared cultural patterns while still referring to something the culture understood as real. He therefore avoids equating myth with falsehood or with purely symbolic speech. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5499" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:39</a> | In Genesis 6:1-5, was it a sin for angels to marry human women? | He said he does not think they were angels; "sons of God" can mean other roles, and angels do not marry or procreate. | He answers by rejecting the angelic reading. In his view, "sons of God" need not mean angels, and Jesus' teaching about angels not marrying weighs against the idea of angel-human marriages. The sin in Genesis 6 is therefore not framed as angels marrying women, but as another kind of transgression in the passage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5795" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:35</a> | What does it mean to "come down" in the Genesis 6 passage? | He says the text does not say they came down; it says they came in, which he reads as a euphemism for sex. | He corrects the premise by saying Genesis 6 does not use language of descending in that place. The relevant wording is "came in," which he reads as sexual euphemism. That detail matters because he is arguing against importing an angelic descent scenario into the wording of the passage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5855" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:35</a> | Could Genesis 6:3 be translated as God's Spirit striving "in" man forever? | He says "with" is the clearer reading, while stretching it to "in" would not be his choice. | He treats "with" as the more natural rendering of the phrase. Translating it as "in" might be attempted, but he considers that a stretch and not the reading he would choose. The answer is a translation judgment rather than a new theological argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=5918" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:38</a> | Does the text explicitly identify the Nephilim as offspring of the sons of God and daughters of men? | He says yes; the clause implies that the Nephilim resulted from that union. | He reads the syntax as tying the Nephilim to the union described in the verse. The text's wording connects their presence with the sons of God going in to the daughters of men and those women bearing children. He therefore sees the Nephilim as the offspring in view, not merely unrelated figures mentioned in passing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=6082" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:22</a> | Why would the Nephilim's attributes result directly from this union, and why would they be men of renown? | He argues that breaking a religious vow was thought to produce socially disruptive figures, so "men of renown" is negative in context. | He links the Nephilim's characterization to the nature of the transgression he sees in the passage. If sacred figures or priests violated vows through sexual misconduct, the resulting children would be viewed as socially and religiously disruptive. "Men of renown" is therefore not praise in his reading, but a reputation for notorious or destructive status. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=6170" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:42:50</a> | Is "renown" not a good thing in this context? | Correct; he says "men of renown" is not a compliment and "men of infamy" would better convey the idea. | He says the expression should be heard negatively in context. These are not heroic celebrities being admired; they are figures remembered for badness or social harm. His suggested sense is closer to infamous men than honorable men. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=6525" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:45</a> | Is Genesis 6 an example of the sins of the fathers being passed down? | Yes. He says it is one of the original examples: bad priests produce bad sons, and the children become worse than the fathers. | He interprets Genesis 6 as a generational corruption story. The fathers' religious failure produces children who intensify the problem, so the sin has social and familial consequences beyond the first offenders. In that sense, it illustrates the pattern of fathers' sins being carried forward in later generations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=6668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:08</a> | How should Genesis 5:21-24 be interpreted with the clan theory in mind? | He reads Enoch as a clan-ending progenitor whose extraordinary taking by God causes the whole clan history to be credited to him. | He applies his clan-reading approach by treating Enoch as a representative ancestor whose clan identity becomes concentrated in him. If Enoch is taken by God in an extraordinary way, the lineage's memory can be attached to that climactic figure. The passage then records both an individual event and the closure or reattribution of a clan history. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=7003" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:43</a> | In Enoch's day, was his clan not yet named for him? | He says probably not; the earlier name is unknown, and Enoch may be the later figure after whom the clan is remembered. | He says the clan's earlier name is not recoverable from the text. In his model, the group may later be remembered under Enoch's name because Enoch became the decisive or famous figure in the lineage. That means the biblical naming may reflect retrospective memory rather than the clan's original label. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=7120" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:40</a> | Does Genesis 5:24 mean God literally took Enoch to heaven instead of Enoch dying a normal physical death? | Yes; he reads the verse as saying Enoch was taken and did not undergo an ordinary death. | He reads the statement that Enoch was not found because God took him as a real removal from ordinary human death. He does not reduce the verse to a metaphor for normal dying or clan disappearance. The text, in his reading, gives Enoch an exceptional ending. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=7340" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:20</a> | Does "God Most High, creator of heaven and earth" in Genesis 14:19-22 appear in Ugaritic texts? | He is not sure without checking, but says *El Elyon* does not appear in Ugaritic texts while *El* does; Ugaritic evidence is also several centuries later than Abraham. | He answers cautiously and refuses to overclaim without checking. His remembered point is that *El* is present in Ugaritic material, but *El Elyon* is not something he recalls there. He also notes that Ugaritic texts are later than Abraham by several centuries, so they are not direct evidence for Abraham's own period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=7529" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:29</a> | If God's energy can punish wrongdoers in hell, why did God not do that when he created the world? | He says immediate summary judgment would have ended all humanity without grace or mercy, while delayed judgment allows God's mercy and patience to be shown. | He frames the issue around judgment and mercy. If God immediately destroyed every wrongdoer, human history would end almost at once and there would be no space for repentance, patience, or grace. Delayed judgment is therefore not weakness; it is part of God's merciful governance before final punishment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=7846" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:10:46</a> | Does Revelation 1:18 mean Jesus is the warden of hell? | No. He says the keys of death and Hades mean Christ has conquered death and controls release from the realm of the dead, so he is a liberator rather than a warden. | He interprets the keys as authority over death and Hades after Christ's victory. The image is not of Jesus managing a prison for torment, but of Jesus having power to open the realm of the dead and free those held by death. The emphasis is conquest, release, and resurrection authority. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=8044" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:04</a> | If Revelation 1:18 meant hell, would it have said Gehenna? | Not necessarily; he says Gehenna refers to the lake of fire, while Hades is the realm of the dead, and "hell" is a broad later term. | He distinguishes several terms often collapsed into "hell." Hades is the realm of the dead, while Gehenna relates to final fiery judgment or the lake of fire. Because English "hell" can blur those categories, Revelation's use of Hades should not simply be read as Gehenna. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=8334" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:54</a> | Did Egyptian gods get new characteristics or behaviors after a pharaoh, and were new gods constantly being created? | Generally no; he says traits were not borrowed from kings, though cults could borrow traits from other religious movements, and gods were more often added than created. | He says Egyptian gods did not normally take on traits because a pharaoh had them. Divine characteristics could shift through cultic development or interaction with other religious movements, but that is different from gods borrowing behavior from kings. New deities could be added to the system, yet he does not describe a constant creation of brand-new gods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=8850" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:30</a> | How much would it cost to pay you for an academic book review? | He says he is very slow with book reviews and is still working through a promised review, so the answer is effectively that it would take time. | He answers by warning about his pace rather than quoting a price. He says he is slow at book reviews and already has a promised review still unfinished. The practical answer is that payment would not quickly solve the time problem. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=8952" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:12</a> | Out of LDS, fundamentalist, and liberal scholarly circles, which is the worst? | He says it is hard to answer because each has different problems: LDS is outside historic Christianity, fundamentalism is hyperliteral and parochial, and liberal scholarship is not Christian. | He refuses a simple ranking because he sees different errors in each camp. LDS teaching is outside historic Christianity, fundamentalism can be rigid, hyperliteral, and insulated, and liberal scholarship can abandon Christian commitments. His answer is comparative rather than a single "worst" label. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=8998" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:58</a> | How is the video game development coming? | He says it is going well, with five of seven endings finished and more asset work still to do. | He gives a brief progress update on the project. Most of the narrative endings are done, with five out of seven complete, but asset work and remaining development tasks still need attention. The answer is status-oriented, not a release announcement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9058" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:58</a> | Was Joseph Smith's Egyptian grammar on par with the understanding level of his time? | He says it was far below par because Champollion had already deciphered Egyptian, though that knowledge had not reached Joseph Smith's level. | He argues that Joseph Smith's Egyptian work was not merely typical for the best scholarship of the era. Champollion's decipherment had already changed the field, so the scholarly standard was above Smith's grammar. He allows that the knowledge may not have been available to Smith locally, but that does not make his work equal to contemporary Egyptology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9264" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:24</a> | Why do you oppose Christian nationalism conceptually, and how does it differ from medieval systems? | He rejects it as a movement combining legalism and racism, saying it would impose state control on church life and contradict Christian liberty. | He criticizes Christian nationalism as a modern ideological movement, not simply as a return to medieval Christendom. In his view, it tends toward legalism, racialized identity, and state intrusion into the church. He contrasts that with Christian liberty and warns that political coercion would damage rather than protect Christian faithfulness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9390" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:30</a> | What denominations would you consider to be Christian nationalist? | He says he does not know of denominations that are Christian nationalist; he describes the movement as driven by individual platformed voices. | He does not identify whole denominations as Christian nationalist. Instead, he presents the phenomenon as coming from individuals, public figures, or platformed movements that influence churches. That distinction prevents him from labeling entire denominational bodies on the basis of some advocates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9518" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:38:38</a> | Will Christian nationalists be among those who hear "Depart from me, I never knew you"? | He answers yes, then jokes with the wording; the serious direction is that he views the movement as spiritually dangerous. | He answers in the affirmative and then plays with the phrasing, but the serious point is clear. He sees Christian nationalism as spiritually dangerous enough that some adherents could fall under Christ's warning. The answer reflects moral and theological concern rather than a denominational taxonomy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:25</a> | Do the Philistine-inhabited lands roughly correspond to today's Gaza Strip? | No. He says Philistine territory in the Iron Age was much more expansive, extending up the coastal plain and into the western Shephelah. | He rejects equating Philistia with the modern Gaza Strip. The Iron Age Philistine cities and territory extended along a broader coastal plain and into the western Shephelah. Gaza was part of the picture, but the ancient territory was larger than the present strip. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9816" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:36</a> | What extra-biblical information do we have on Abraham's father Terah? | He points to five Mari letters that he says fit Terah's name, time, place, son dying, political turmoil, and sojourner status. | He appeals to a cluster of Mari letters, not a single isolated datum. He says they contain a name form he connects with Terah and line up with several contextual details, including period, location, family circumstance, political disturbance, and sojourning status. The argument is cumulative and presented as extra-biblical support for Terah's historicity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=9926" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:26</a> | Are there counterfactuals or detail mismatches in the Mari evidence for Terah? | He says the main mismatch is Ur/Kesh of the Chaldees, which may be an anachronism, misspelling, or spacing issue that can be resolved. | He acknowledges that the evidence is not frictionless. The biggest issue he names concerns the place wording connected with Ur or Kesh of the Chaldees. He suggests that this may be explained by anachronism, scribal or spelling issues, or spacing, so he does not treat it as fatal to the identification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10009" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:49</a> | Do the Mari letters mention Terah by name? | Yes. He says the letters use a form like Teru, which he treats as Terah with the expected terminal consonant. | He says the letters include a name form he vocalizes or explains as Teru, which he connects to Terah. The difference is not treated as decisive because the relevant terminal consonant or form can fit the expected name pattern. His answer is confident that the name itself is part of the evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10153" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:13</a> | Do you think the Jasher tablet is fake? | He thinks it probably is, but says he would need to look closely; he distinguishes that tablet from other evidence for Jasher. | He gives a cautious negative judgment. The specific Jasher tablet sounds fake to him, but he says he would need to examine it closely before making a firm claim. He also separates that object from broader discussions of Jasher, so skepticism about the tablet does not settle every Jasher-related question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10192" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:52</a> | What is the difference between early biblical history and biblical prehistory? | He says history is defined by written records or external sources, so figures attested in letters move from prehistory into history. | He defines the boundary by external written attestation. Material without written records belongs more naturally to prehistory, while persons or events attested in letters or other documents enter the realm of history. On that basis, extra-biblical references can move early biblical figures from prehistory into historical discussion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10270" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:10</a> | What are your thoughts on Benjamin Sommer's claim that God has multiple fluid and sometimes simultaneous bodies? | He has not read the work closely enough to comment, but urges caution because that conclusion can get weird quickly. | He explicitly limits his answer because he has not read Sommer's work closely enough to evaluate it. His only substantive response is caution: claims about God having multiple or simultaneous bodies can move into strange or theologically difficult territory quickly. He does not offer a detailed critique beyond that caveat. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10330" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:10</a> | Would oral history be considered prehistory? | Often, but not always; he says oral history can overlap with recorded history and is not mutually exclusive with it. | He says oral history often belongs to prehistory because it may lack written attestation, but the categories are not mutually exclusive. Oral traditions can continue alongside written records, and historical periods can preserve oral material. The classification depends on the evidence available, not on oral transmission alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10401" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:21</a> | Did Jewish priests take a vow of silence, as with Zacharias in Luke? | He says Torah and later texts do not describe such a priestly vow, so Zacharias's silence appears unique or unexplained. | He says he does not know of a Torah command or later Jewish practice in which priests took a vow of silence like Zacharias's condition. The silence in Luke therefore should not be assumed to reflect a normal priestly ritual. It appears to be a unique narrative feature or an unexplained circumstance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10478" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:38</a> | Is Canaan the product of Ham and Noah's wife, explaining Noah's curse, and is this a clan-usurpation attempt? | He says Canaan is the product of that act and that explains the curse; clan usurpation is possible, but not certain. | He reads the Noah episode as involving sexual wrongdoing with Noah's wife, making Canaan the product of that act and explaining why Canaan is cursed. He allows that clan usurpation may be part of the social logic, but he does not present it as certain. The answer combines a direct textual reading with a cautious social-historical possibility. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10830" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:30</a> | What about Shem and Japheth walking backward; would just looking be considered a sex act? | He says looking would still be an act of lewdness toward the naked woman involved, and the backward walking indicates they were covering a woman. | He says the backward walking matters because it shows they are avoiding a lewd exposure situation, not merely dealing with a sleeping naked man. In his reading, the nakedness involved is that of the woman, and even looking would be sexually improper. Their behavior marks them as refusing participation in the violation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=10891" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:31</a> | Would it be sinful to rewrite the Bible into modern language and remove the euphemisms? | No. He says removing euphemisms and updating contextual readings could be good, though controversial. | He says a modernized rendering that makes euphemisms explicit would not be sinful in itself. It could even help readers understand what the text is communicating, as long as the translation is responsible. He also notes that such a project would be controversial because many readers are attached to traditional softened wording. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=11230" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:10</a> | Who do you think the historical Amalekites were? | Tentatively, he identifies them with the Lukka, a Sea Peoples group, but says the argument still needs to be written and published. | He offers the Lukka identification as a tentative thesis rather than a settled published conclusion. In his view, the Amalekites may be connected with this Sea Peoples group, but the argument still needs to be written up and defended. He therefore marks the answer as provisional. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=11384" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:44</a> | If the Amalekites were Indo-European, did they have a language related to modern English? | Only distantly; he says English is Germanic, while the Amalekites would more likely be closer to Hittite or another Anatolian Indo-European branch. | He explains that "Indo-European" is a broad family label, not a claim of close relation to English. English belongs to the Germanic branch, whereas a Lukka or Anatolian connection would place Amalekite language much closer to Hittite-type material. Any relationship to English would therefore be distant and family-level. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=11520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:12:00</a> | Will your newly published chronology paper be available on Sci-Hub? | No; he says it will be open source somewhere, but he still needs details, and he will also post a copy on Patreon. | He says the paper should be openly available through legitimate means rather than needing Sci-Hub. He is still unsure of the exact publication access details, but expects an open-source version and plans to share a copy on Patreon. The answer is about access logistics, not the content of the paper. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=11641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:01</a> | Do you plan to share this chronology research at the next Evangelical Theological Society conference? | No, because he says it is a paper on chronology and chronology methods, not theology. | He says he would not present this work at ETS because the paper is about chronology and method rather than theology proper. The venue mismatch is the reason, not unwillingness to discuss the work. He treats it as more appropriate for a field connected to dating and archaeological method. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=11793" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:33</a> | Is Archaeometry a famous academic journal? | Yes. He identifies the journal as *Archaeometry* and treats it as a significant academic venue. | He confirms that *Archaeometry* is a real and significant journal. In context, he treats publication there as academically meaningful for the kind of scientific and chronological work being discussed. The answer is brief but affirmative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12028" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:28</a> | Why don't researchers expose peer reviewers who are insulting like that? | He says they sometimes do, especially when reviewers reveal their names despite nominal anonymity. | He says exposure can happen, especially when peer reviewers identify themselves or otherwise make anonymity ineffective. The peer-review system is supposed to be anonymous in many cases, but that does not always hold. When insulting reviewers are identifiable, researchers sometimes do make the behavior public. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12093" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:33</a> | Did the Hittites' use of "king of Ahhiyawa" suggest a lack of understanding of Greek political culture, and are there parallels elsewhere? | Yes. He says cross-cultural diplomatic misunderstandings happened among Hittites, Egyptians, Assyrians, Mitanni, Greeks, and others. | He says the title can reflect a foreign court imposing its own diplomatic categories onto another culture. The Hittites could speak of a "king of Ahhiyawa" even if Greek political organization did not match that model exactly. He notes that similar misunderstandings or simplifications happened among several ancient diplomatic powers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12344" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:44</a> | Is the Ipuwer Papyrus an accurate depiction of the Exodus? | No. He says it describes earlier plague-like hypernatural events, not the Exodus itself, which he places centuries later. | He rejects identifying Ipuwer directly with the exodus. The text may preserve or describe earlier plague-like, hypernatural disaster traditions, but he places the exodus itself much later. It can be relevant background for Egyptian catastrophe language without being a report of Moses' exodus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12500" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:20</a> | Does Heiser's holy stapler analogy discredit the prophetic nature of Isaiah 53? | He passes because he does not know that analogy well enough to comment. | He declines to answer substantively because he does not know the specific Heiser analogy well enough. Rather than speculate, he passes on the question. The only grounded answer here is that he offers no evaluation of Isaiah 53 on the basis of that analogy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12536" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:56</a> | Could you explain Leviticus 19:2 in context with the rest of chapter 19? | He says holiness in Leviticus 19 means keeping the law, doing justice, obeying applicable ritual law, and maintaining righteousness. | He explains "be holy" through the chapter's own commands rather than as an abstract feeling of separateness. Leviticus 19 ties holiness to concrete obedience, including justice, right treatment of others, and ritual obligations that applied under the law. Holiness is therefore shown in covenantal righteousness and practice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=12883" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:43</a> | How can someone in third-year Greek learn to care about old languages? | He advises treating study as worship and remembering that the usefulness of Greek often becomes clear after seminary through later reading and ministry. | He counsels the student to connect language study to worship and service rather than only to grades. Greek may feel tedious or useless during coursework, but its value often becomes clearer later when reading Scripture, doing ministry, or handling texts responsibly. The discipline is worth cultivating even when motivation lags. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=13154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:14</a> | Should Greek still be confusing in third year? | Yes. He says continued confusion means the student is seeing the real difficulties of advanced Greek, especially with genitives and participles. | He normalizes confusion at that stage. Early Greek may give a sense of competence, but advanced work exposes difficult constructions, especially genitives and participles. Being confused in third year can mean the student is finally encountering the real complexity of the language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=13232" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:40:32</a> | Is Hebrew easier than Greek? | He says Hebrew is harder for the first couple of months because of the script and Masoretic points, but the grammar gets easier over time, while Greek gets harder. | He compares the learning curves. Hebrew starts hard because students must learn a new script and pointing system, but its grammar becomes more manageable after the initial barrier. Greek may feel easier at first, but later syntax and advanced categories make it increasingly difficult. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=13346" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:26</a> | If someone plans to teach Old Testament at seminary, will Hebrew be heavily required and will Greek still matter? | Yes. He says Hebrew is essential, comparative languages may matter, and Greek still matters for Septuagint and textual criticism work. | He says an Old Testament teacher needs serious Hebrew and may also need related ancient Near Eastern languages depending on specialization. Greek still matters because the Septuagint and textual criticism are important for Old Testament study. The field is not Hebrew-only, even though Hebrew is central. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=13821" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:21</a> | What is the biggest "I told you so" moment in biblical archaeology? | He points to the Tel Dan Stele, especially its reference to the house of David against earlier minimalist denials of a Davidic kingdom. | He names the Tel Dan Stele because it directly challenged claims that David or a Davidic dynasty lacked historical grounding. The "house of David" reference became a major piece of external evidence against stronger minimalist positions. For him, it is a clear example where archaeology forced a reassessment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=13936" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:16</a> | Do you think scholars will ever have a similar "I told you so" moment with Moses? | It is possible, but he is not optimistic about a direct smoking-gun Moses inscription because of how the exodus worked. | He allows the possibility of new evidence but is cautious. A direct inscription naming Moses would be unlikely because of the nature of the exodus and the contexts in which evidence survives. He is more open to indirect corroboration than to a simple decisive artifact. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=14104" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:04</a> | Have you seen the Joe Rogan interview with Zahi Hawass? | No. He says he has not seen it and avoids saying much about Hawass because Hawass still has power in Egypt. | He says he has not watched the interview. He also avoids extended comment on Hawass because Hawass remains influential in Egyptian archaeology and heritage institutions. The answer is deliberately restrained. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=14183" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:56:23</a> | Is there a book recommendation on different views of the supplementary hypothesis compared with the documentary hypothesis? | He does not know of a good book yet, but recommends the writings of Benjamin Kilchör, whom he identifies as a supplementary-hypothesis proponent and documentary-hypothesis critic. | He cannot name a single go-to book for the comparison. Instead, he recommends looking at Benjamin Kilchör, whom he describes as a scholar associated with a supplementary-hypothesis approach and criticism of the documentary hypothesis. The recommendation is author-based rather than title-based. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=14231" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:11</a> | Is Zahi Hawass Muslim? | Yes. | He gives a brief affirmative answer and does not expand it into a broader discussion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=14248" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:57:28</a> | Does Jonah 2:2-7 show that Jonah died inside the fish, especially since similar language appears in Psalm 30? | He says Jonah 2 does present Jonah as descending to death, while Psalm 30 uses similar imagery differently and should not be flattened into the same meaning. | He reads Jonah 2 as death language in Jonah's specific narrative context, with Jonah descending toward or into the realm of death. Psalm 30 uses similar rescue imagery, but he warns against assuming the same phrases must mean the same thing in both places. Context determines whether the language is literal death, near death, or poetic deliverance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/H6CBCG9YX4U?t=14959" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:09:19</a> | Does the people determinative with "Israel" on the Merneptah Stele show that Merneptah encountered Israel away from its home territory? | No; he says the people determinative alone does not prove that, but the throwing-stick determinative and the poetic foreign-nations context do support Israel being treated as a foreign people group. | He says the people determinative by itself only marks Israel as a people, not necessarily as a people encountered away from home. The stronger evidence is the broader determinative and literary context, including foreign-enemy framing in the poem. That supports Israel being treated as a foreign people group in Merneptah's campaign rhetoric, but not the specific claim made from the people determinative alone. |
